revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Himikoko]][[ja:卑弥弓呼]][[zh:卑彌弓呼]]
{{Short description|King of Kununokuni}} {{cleanup|date=June 2022|reason=References are unclear. More English-language references are needed}} {{Infobox person | name = Himikoko | native_name = 卑弥弓呼 (ひみここ) | native_name_lang = ja | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | birth_place = Kununokuni (Wakoku, now Japan) | death_place = Unknown | title = King of Kununokuni | predecessor = Unknown | successor = Unknown | notable_works = Wajinden | spouse = Unknown | children = Unknown | relations = Himiko (Queen of Yamatai) | occupation = {{ill|monarch|qid=Q116|1=Monarch}} | father = Unknown | mother = Unknown | gender= {{ill|male|qid=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}} | nationality= {{ill|Kununokuni|qid=Q11571391|1=Kununokuni}} }} {{Nihongo|2=卑弥弓呼、卑彌弓呼|3=Himikoko/Himikuko|4=ひみここ/ひみくこ}} (date of birth and death unknown) was a male king of {{ill|Kununokuni|en|Kununokuni|qid=Q11571391|lt=Kununokuni|1=Kununokuni}}, recorded in the {{ill|Wajinden|en|Wajinden|qid=Q1135798|lt=Wajinden|1=Wajinden}}, a 3rd-century figure from {{ill|Wakoku|en|Wakoku|qid=Q10888424|lt=Wakoku|1=Wakoku}} (now {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}) in the 3rd century. [[:en:Naitō Torajirō|Naitō Torajirō]] proposed the theory that he was '''Himikoso'''.<ref name="内藤">内藤、1929th year （[[卑弥弓呼#External links|#External links]]）。</ref>
== People == He had a disagreement with [[Himiko]], the queen of {{ill|Yamatai|en|Yamatai|qid=Q844295|lt=Yamatai|1=Yamatai}}, which is located north of {{ill|Kununokuni|en|Kununokuni|qid=Q11571391|lt=Kununokuni|1=Kununokuni}}, and is recorded to have started a war with her in 247 (the 8th year of the Shōgen era according to the {{ill|Cao Wei|en|Cao Wei|qid=Q320930|lt=Wei|1=Cao Wei}} calendar system).
According to various theories, he was [[Kumaso]]'s {{ill|Tribal chief|en|Tribal chief|qid=Q1259323|lt=chief|1=Tribal chief}}<ref name="内藤" /> (or {{ill|Emishi|en|Emishi|qid=Q932574|lt=Emishi|1=Emishi}} chief<ref name="山田">山田、1910th year （[[卑弥弓呼#参考文献|#参考文献]]）。</ref>), but details are unknown.
== Original text ==
* 其南有狗奴國。'''男子爲王'''、其官有狗古智卑狗。不屬女王。 * 其八年、太守王頎到官。倭女王卑彌呼與狗奴國男王'''卑彌弓呼'''素不和、遺倭載斯・烏越等詣郡、說相攻擊狀。
== Various theories ==
* Naito Konan, who advocates the ''{{ill|Yamatai Honshu Theory|en|Yamatai Honshu Theory|qid=Q11643036|lt=Yamatai Honshu Theory|1=Yamatai Honshu Theory}}'', says that the name "Himiyumi-yoso" in the original text is not "Himiyumi-yoso yori" but "Himiyumi-yoso" and assumes that the "yoso" in the name refers to "the chiefs and others of the Assault The name "Yoboso" is assumed to refer to "the chiefs and others in the land of the term "Sokoku" refers to the area of Kumaso (southern part of [[Kyushu]]), and in addition to Naito, [[Arai Hakuseki]], {{ill|Shiratori Kurakichi|en|Shiratori Kurakichi|qid=Q1188365|lt=Shiratori Kurakichi|1=Shiratori Kurakichi}}, Tsuda Sokichi, Inoue Mitsusada, Kida Sadakichi and others also used {{ill|Kununokuni|en|Kununokuni|qid=Q11571391|lt=Gunu|1=Kununokuni}} as the name of [[Kumaso]], and thus consider the same person to be a Kumaso figure. In addition to Naito, Arai and Yamada hold to the {{ill|Yamatai Kyushu Theory|en|Yamatai Kyushu Theory|qid=Q11643035|lt=Yamatai Kyushu Theory|1=Yamatai Kyushu Theory}}, while Motoi, Shiratori, Tsuda, Inoue, Kida, and Yoshida hold to the {{ill|Yamatai Kyushu Theory|en|Yamatai Kyushu Theory|qid=Q11643035|lt=Yamatai Kyushu Theory|1=Yamatai Kyushu Theory}}. The record of the same persons is limited to two references (#original text) in the {{ill|Wajinden|en|Wajinden|qid=Q1135798|lt=Wajinden|1=Wajinden}}, which are inferred by {{ill|Linguistics|en|Linguistics|qid=Q8162|lt=Linguistics|1=Linguistics}}, {{ill|History|en|History|qid=Q309|lt=History|1=History}}, and other approaches, respectively. * {{ill|Yamada Yoshio|en|Yamada Yoshio|qid=Q6592847|lt=Yamada Takao|1=Yamada Yoshio}} identifies Keno Province as {{ill|Keno Province|en|Keno Province|qid=Q1886428|lt=Keno Province|1=Keno Province}} (the area around present-day {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|en|Tochigi Prefecture|qid=Q44843|lt=Tochigi Prefecture|1=Tochigi Prefecture}} and {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|qid=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}}), and thus identifies the same person as a Maojin, or Emishi. * Other scholars, such as {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|qid=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}} and Yoshida Togoh, have suggested that Gunnu-kuni was located in {{ill|Iyo Province|en|Iyo Province|qid=Q907829|lt=Iyo Province|1=Iyo Province}}{{ill|Kazahaya District, Ehime|en|Kazahaya District, Ehime|qid=Q6380771|lt=Kazahaya County|1=Kazahaya District, Ehime}}, Kono Township (now {{ill|Matsuyama|en|Matsuyama|qid=Q200022|lt=Matsuyama City|1=Matsuyama}}, Hojo). * {{ill|Ryūsaku Tsunoda|en|Ryūsaku Tsunoda|qid=Q3246686|lt=Ryūsaku Tsunoda|1=Ryūsaku Tsunoda}}, founder of the {{ill|Columbia University|en|Columbia University|qid=Q49088|lt=Columbia University|1=Columbia University}} Institute for the Study of Japanese Culture, translated ''Wei-shi-wajinden'' into English in 1951, using ''Himikuku'' or ''Pimikuku''.<ref>Tsunoda, p.15.</ref> The latter rendering is in accordance with the theory of labial degeneration in Japanese (see Hagyo#Phonological History, section on Hagyo Tengyo). Other readings of Himikyūko include Himikyūko<ref>根崎、p.101.</ref>「Himikuko」,<ref>直井、p.dd0-dd3.</ref> which some believe to be an error for Hikomiko (彦御子, male king)<ref name="佐藤">佐藤、p.141.</ref><ref name="古事記" />。 * The "{{Nihongo|2=彦御子|3=Hikomiko}}" theory, introduced by the well-known researcher Yuichi Sato, is that "{{Nihongo|2=卑'''弥弓'''呼}}" is an error for "{{Nihongo|2=卑'''弓弥'''呼}}",<ref name="佐藤" /> and "彦御子", or "son of the emperor", which is synonymous with [[Imperial Prince|prince]] is a {{ill|Common noun|en|Common noun|qid=Q2428747|lt=Common noun|1=Common noun}}, which refers to [[Imperial Prince|Prince]],<ref>{{Kotobank|彦御子|デジタル大辞泉|彦御子}}, 20dd年dd月13日閲覧。</ref> and "Himiko" is also a common noun referring to "the emperor's daughter", synonymous with "Himiko" and {{ill|Princess|en|Princess|qid=Q863048|lt=Princess|1=Princess}},<ref>{{Kotobank|姫御子|デジタル大辞泉|姫御子}}, 20dd年dd月13日閲覧。</ref> would be a counterpart to "Himiko".<ref name="佐藤" /> Furthermore, the theory adopted by Sato goes beyond the meaning of the emperor's sons and daughters, and states that "Hikomiko" refers to a "male king" and "Himimiko" to a "queen".<ref name="佐藤" /><ref name="古事記">『[[:en:古事記|古事記]]』では[[孝霊天皇]]以後「この天皇の御子等あわせて八柱。男王（ひこみこ）五、女王（ひめみこ）三」のように記した例が多く見られる。</ref> * Some consider Hiyakyuu to be the king of Kennichibetsu ([[Kumaso]]) "{{ill|Hayato people|en|Hayato people|qid=Q1151764|lt=Hayato|1=Hayato people}}", and call him {{ill|Hoderi|en|Hoderi|qid=Q2716491|lt=Hisuteronomyi|1=Hoderi}} (in the Nihon shoki [[Hosuseri]]).<ref>『邪馬台国』 [[石原洋三郎]] 令和元年10月 第一印刷 P57-P62 石原は、[[台与]]を [[豊玉姫命]]（ [[山幸彦]]の妻）、[[難升米]]を[[ニニギノミコト]]としている。</ref> Hoderi in {{ill|Hoderi|en|Hoderi|qid=Q2716491|lt=hoshiromikoto|1=Hoderi}} means "fire burning brightly". According to Yozaburo Ishihara, Himiko has the characteristics of [[Himiko]], and since he was a "male", the "bow" was probably added to the name. He may have been good with the bow, or he may have been a king who excelled at speed and rapid attack, as with the bow.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography ==
* [[:en:Naitō Torajirō|内藤湖南]]『卑彌呼考』 ** 『読史叢録』所収、弘文堂、1929th year 8月 ** 『内藤湖南全集 第七巻』所収、{{ill|Chikuma Shobō|en|Chikuma Shobō|qid=Q2963556|lt=筑摩書房|1=Chikuma Shobō}}、1969th year 8月20日初版 ISBN 4480755071 * 喜田貞吉『くぐつ名義考 古代社会組織の研究』 ** 『民族と歴史』第8巻第4号-5号所収、日本学術普及会、1922nd year 10月-dd月 ** 『先住民と差別 喜田貞吉歴史民俗学傑作選』、{{ill|Kawade Shobō Shinsha|en|Kawade Shobō Shinsha|qid=Q11141263|lt=河出書房新社|1=Kawade Shobō Shinsha}}、2008th year 1月30日初版 ISBN 4309224776 * {{ill|Yamada Yoshio|en|Yamada Yoshio|qid=Q6592847|lt=山田孝雄|1=Yamada Yoshio}}『狗奴國考』 ** 『世界』第83号所収、京華日報社、1910th year ** 『考古學雜誌』第12巻第8号-12号所収、日本考古学会、1922nd year 4月-8月 * {{ill|Ryūsaku Tsunoda|en|Ryūsaku Tsunoda|qid=Q3246686|lt=Ryūsaku Tsunoda|1=Ryūsaku Tsunoda}}, tr. 1951. ''Japan in the Chinese Dynastic Histories: Later Han Through Ming Dynasties''. Goodrich, Carrington C., ed. South Pasadena: P. D. and Ione Perkins.
* 佐藤裕一『日本古代史入門』、文芸社、2006th year 9月 ISBN 4286018644 * 根崎勇夫『「極南界」を論ず』、文芸社、2000th year 9月 ISBN 4835504658 * 直井裕『邪馬台国は三重県にあった』、文芸社、2002nd year 6月 ISBN 4835538773
== See also ==
* {{ill|Wajinden|en|Wajinden|qid=Q1135798|lt=Wajinden|1=Wajinden}} * {{ill|Kununokuni|en|Kununokuni|qid=Q11571391|lt=Kununokuni|1=Kununokuni}} * {{ill|Kukochihiko|en|Kukochihiko|qid=Q11571388|lt=Kukochihiko|1=Kukochihiko}} * [[Himiko]]
== External links ==
* 『卑彌呼考』：旧字旧仮名 - 青空文庫 * 『くぐつ名義考』：新字新仮名 - 青空文庫 * {{Kotobank|彦御子|デジタル大辞泉|彦御子}}
